perturbation.original_text: 5. (a) For PCII - (1) Upon the completion of my engagement as an employee, consultant, or subcontractor under the contract, or the completion of my work on the PCII Program, whichever occurs first, I will surrender promptlyto the PCII Program Manager or his designee, or to the appropriate PCII officer, PCII of any type whatsoeverthat is in my possession.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. (a) For PCII - (1) Upon the completion of my engagement as an employee, consultant, or subcontractor under the contract, or the completion of my work on the PCII Program, whichever occurs first, I will surrender PCII of any type whatsoever that is in my possession at my discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the text to allow the individual to surrender PCII at their discretion contradicts the mandatory surrender requirement, potentially violating regulations concerning the protection of critical infrastructure information.
perturbation.law_explanation: 6 CFR § 29.8(a) mandates safeguarding PCII and restricts its use. Allowing discretionary surrender violates the requirement to protect and control PCII, potentially leading to unauthorized disclosure.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the surrender of PCII upon completion of engagement or work. The changed text makes this surrender discretionary ('at my discretion'), directly contradicting the mandatory requirement. The Law Explanation points to 6 CFR § 29.8(a), which mandates safeguarding PCII. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 6 CFR Part 29, supports this regulation by outlining the purpose and scope of protected critical infrastructure information, indicating that the discretionary surrender weakens the protections afforded to PCII under federal regulations, creating a potential risk of unauthorized disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: 7. I hereby agree that I shall promptly report to the appropriate official, in accordance with the guidance issued for the applicable category of information, any loss, theft, misuse, misplacement, unauthorized disclosure, or other securityviolation, I have knowledge of and whether or not I am personally involved. I also understand that my anonymity will bekept to the extent possible when reporting security violations.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. I hereby agree that I shall report any loss, theft, misuse, misplacement, unauthorized disclosure, or other security violation, I have knowledge of and whether or not I am personally involved, only if I believe the violation is significant.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying or preventing the report of security violations based on personal assessment conflicts with the requirement for prompt reporting and might violate laws related to data breach notification and security incident reporting for federal information systems.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 44 U.S. Code § 3554 - Federal information security management
perturbation.law_explanation: 44 U.S. Code § 3554(b)(4) mandates agencies to have procedures for detecting, reporting, and responding to security incidents. Delaying or preventing the report of security violations if it is not believed to be significant conflicts with the incident reporting requirements.

Federal agency responsibilities (a) In General .-The head of each agency shall- (1) be responsible for- (A) providing information security protections commensurate with the risk and magnitude of the harm resulting from unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction of- (i) information collected or maintained by or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a subjective element ('only if I believe the violation is significant') that directly undermines the original requirement for prompt reporting of security violations. This contradicts the mandate in 44 U.S. Code § 3554(b)(4), which the Law Explanation states requires agencies to have procedures for detecting, reporting, and responding to security incidents, implying a comprehensive and immediate reporting obligation. The scraped law snippet 1, referencing 44 USC 3554, further emphasizes federal agency responsibilities regarding information security protections commensurate with the risk. The modification allows for personal assessment to potentially delay or prevent reporting, creating a legal contradiction because it deviates from the expected and legally required immediate reporting.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Unless and until I am released in writing by an authorized representative of the Department of Homeland Security (if permissible for the particular category of information), I understand that all conditions and obligations imposed upon me bythis Agreement apply during the time that I am granted conditional access, and at all times thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Unless and until I am released in writing by an authorized representative of the Department of Homeland Security (if permissible for the particular category of information), I understand that all conditions and obligations imposed upon me bythis Agreement apply only during the time that I am granted conditional access, and not at any time thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the obligations to only the period of access and not thereafter contradicts the perpetual obligation to protect sensitive information, possibly violating regulations concerning the safeguarding of classified or sensitive information even after access is terminated.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Executive Order 13526, Section 5.5 - Duration of classification
perturbation.law_explanation: Executive Order 13526, Section 5.5 establishes that classified information remains classified as long as required by national security. This directly contradicts the agreement's limitation of confidentiality obligations to only the active access period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Executive Order 13526 [Executive Orders] PDF Version In-Line Edit Version Executive Order 13526 of December 29, 2009 The White House Office of the Press Secretary For Immediate Release December 29, 2009 Executive Order - Classified National Security Information This order prescribes a uniform system for classifying, safeguarding, and declassifying national security information, including information relating to defense against transnational terrorism. Our democratic principles require that the American people be informed of the activities of their Government. Also, our Nation's progress depends on the free flow of information both within the Government and to the American people. Nevertheless, throughout our history, the national defense has required that certain information be maintained in confidence in order to protect our citizens, our democratic institutions, our homeland security, and our interactions with foreign nations. Protecting information critical to our Nation's security and demonstrating our commitment to open Government through accurate and accountable application of classification standards and routine, secure, and effective declassification are equally important priorities. NOW, THEREFORE, I, BARACK OBAMA, by the authority vested in me as President by the Constitution and the laws of the United States of America, it is hereby ordered as follows: PART 1 -- ORIGINAL CLASSIFICATION Section 1.1. Classification Standards. (a) Information may be originally classified under the terms of this order only if all of the following conditions are met: (1) an original classification authority is classifying the information; (2) the information is owned by, produced by or for, or is under the control of the United States Government; (3) the information falls within one or more of the categories of information listed in section 1.4 of this order; and (4) the original classification authority determines that the unauthorized disclosure of the information reasonably could be expected to
perturbation.justification: The modified text limits the obligations to only the period of access, whereas the original text extends the obligation at all times thereafter. The Law Explanation states Executive Order 13526, Section 5.5 establishes that classified information remains classified as long as required by national security, contradicting the agreement's limitation of confidentiality obligations to only the active access period. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing Executive Order 13526, discusses safeguarding national security information. This supports the conclusion that the modified text contradicts the ongoing obligation to protect classified information, as the modified agreement limits this obligation, while the law requires it to persist as long as national security requires. This creates a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 14. These restrictions are consistent with and do not supersede, conflict with, or otherwise alter the employee obligations, rights, or liabilities created by Executive Order No. 12958, as amended; Section 7211 of Title 5, UnitedStates Code (governing disclosures to Congress); Section 1034 of Title 10, United States Code, as amended by theMilitary Whistleblower Protection Act (governing disclosure to Congress by members of the military); Section2302(b)(8) of Title 5, United States Code, as amended by the Whistleblower Protection Act (governing disclosures ofillegality, waste, fraud, abuse or public health or safety threats); the Intelligence Identities Protection Act of 1982 (50USC 421 et seq.) (governing disclosures that could expose confidential Government agents); and the statutes whichprotect against disclosure that may compromise the national security, including Sections 641, 793, 794, 798, and 952of Title 18, United States Code, and Section 4(b) of the Subversive Activities Act of 1950 (50 USC 783(b)). Thedefinitions, requirements, obligations, rights, sanctions, and liabilities created by said Executive Order and listedstatutes are incorporated into this agreement and are controlling.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. These restrictions supersede the employee obligations, rights, or liabilities created by Section 2302(b)(8) of Title 5, United States Code, as amended by the Whistleblower Protection Act (governing disclosures of illegality, waste, fraud, abuse or public health or safety threats).
perturbation.explanation: Stating that the restrictions supersede whistleblower protection violates federal law protecting government employees who report waste, fraud, and abuse.
perturbation.law_explanation: 5 U.S. Code § 2302(b)(8) protects federal employees from retaliation for disclosing violations of law, waste, fraud, or abuse. Explicitly superseding these protections violates this statute and could create legal liability.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts 5 U.S. Code § 2302(b)(8) by stating that the restrictions supersede whistleblower protection. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that this violates federal law protecting government employees who report waste, fraud, and abuse. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 5 USC 2302, indicating 'Prohibited personnel practices' are defined within, which includes retaliating against whistleblowers. Therefore, superseding these protections is a clear violation.
